Because of additive toxicity, FOSCAVIR should generally be avoided with aminoglycosides or amphotericin B.
Antacids and H 2 -receptor antagonists slightly enhance the absorption of dirithromycin.
Bromocriptine mesylate may interact with dopamine antagonists and butyrophenones, although the interaction is not fully characterized.
An interaction between EPA supplements and aspirin was discussed during counseling, but the patient had stopped the supplements weeks earlier and no bruising occurred.
Bentiromide testing was postponed until after lidocaine had worn off, so no drug interference with the result was observed.

Although the chart carried a warning about FOSCAVIR with aminoglycosides, the patient never received an aminoglycoside.

During bentiromide testing, lidocaine had been used locally, but the specimen was mishandled and the cause of the abnormal result remained uncertain.
After bromocriptine briefly overlapped with haloperidol, symptom control worsened, although progression of the underlying disease made any interaction uncertain.
Blood pressure rose while diethylpropion and guanethidine were both listed, yet poor adherence prevented the team from establishing definite pharmacologic interference.

Among aspirin, clopidogrel, and EPA supplements, only aspirin and the supplements were specifically identified in the chart as interacting; clopidogrel, though continued, was not part of that warning.

Before the dental procedure, nabumetone was continued, warfarin dosing left unchanged, and an antacid given later; only the nabumetone-warfarin combination prompted a cautionary note because similar interactions have been seen with NSAIDs.

The pharmacist documented that diethylpropion may interfere with antihypertensive therapy, particularly guanethidine and a-methyldopa, whereas the patient's insulin requirements were being adjusted for unrelated reasons.

Although the medication guide first frames this as a class warning for dopamine antagonists, in this admission it was metoclopramide, not the previously stopped ondansetron, that clearly diminished the response to bromocriptine.
Warfarin should be used cautiously with CELEBREX because this combination places patients at greater risk for bleeding complications.
Fluoxetine increases atomoxetine exposure through inhibition of CYP2D6-mediated metabolism.
When aspirin is administered with CELEBREX, the rate of gastrointestinal ulceration and related complications may increase.
Tricyclic antidepressants and SSRIs are recognized as interacting drug classes, although the precise mechanism may differ by agent.
Because aspirin had already been stopped before CELEBREX was started, the later gastrointestinal bleeding was not attributed to concomitant therapy.
The patient reported dyspnea while nitrofurantoin appeared on the medication list, but the prescription had never been taken.
Although paroxetine was discussed, atomoxetine was continued alone and its plasma concentrations remained unchanged.
Warfarin was monitored after nabumetone had been discontinued, and no interaction-related INR change was observed.
Heparin was withheld throughout the episode, so the documented hyperkalemia could not be attributed to Heparin exposure.
Bromocriptine was continued during admission, but no dopamine antagonist was administered.
FOSCAVIR and intravenous pentamidine were both mentioned in the consultation note, yet only FOSCAVIR was actually given.
A warning about tetracyclines with acitretin was documented, but the patient received topical clindamycin instead of any tetracycline.
Mild bruising appeared in a patient taking EPA supplements with aspirin, but the episode was brief and the team could not determine whether the combination was clinically relevant.
After CELEBREX was introduced in a patient on warfarin, the INR rose slightly once, although diet change and missed doses made the signal inconclusive.
Respiratory symptoms developed after nitrofurantoin exposure, but pulmonary edema on imaging prevented a definite diagnosis of acute pulmonary reactions.
Atomoxetine levels seemed higher after Fluoxetine was added, yet the sample timing was unreliable and the interaction remained unconfirmed.
Weakness worsened after colchicine was added to simvastatin, although the clinicians favored steroid myopathy over a medication-related effect.
Potassium increased during Heparin therapy, but the patient was also receiving trimethoprim and had worsening renal function, so causality remained uncertain.
The chart mentioned a possible interaction between bromocriptine and metoclopramide, but symptom fluctuation before both drugs made the effect difficult to prove.
While aspirin was given with CELEBREX, abdominal discomfort occurred without endoscopic evidence of ulceration, leaving the contribution of the drug pair uncertain.

Not fluconazole, which had been stopped the week before, but paroxetine, started with atomoxetine on the same day, produced the rise in atomoxetine exposure that led clinicians to reduce the atomoxetine dose.
Although the admission note grouped aspirin, clopidogrel, and warfarin together, only aspirin given with CELEBREX was specifically linked to gastrointestinal ulceration.
The order set warned generally about dopamine antagonists, yet in this patient it was haloperidol, not the previously discontinued prochlorperazine, that reduced the effect of bromocriptine.

Because methotrexate clearance had already been borderline, once Piperacillin sodium was continued after surgery, the delayed excretion became clinically important and repeated methotrexate monitoring was ordered.
Even though only class language was used initially, the final note concluded that EPA supplements interacted with aspirin rather than with the multivitamin started the same week.

The regimen included CELEBREX, lisinopril, and furosemide; while the chart first blamed dehydration, later review attributed the reduced diuretic response to ibuprofen given concurrently with furosemide.
Linezolid has recognized interaction potential with adrenergic and serotonergic agents, and sympathomimetics may show an exaggerated pressor response during coadministration.
Bromocriptine can interact with dopamine antagonists broadly, while haloperidol is one example that can blunt bromocriptine efficacy.
Rifampin and warfarin are a known interacting pair, and rifampin enzyme induction may increase the warfarin dose needed to keep the INR therapeutic.
